# tv — Terminal for Agents (Reference)

tv gives agents their own persistent terminal sessions. Run commands, read
output, and control interactive programs — all programmatically.
Run `tv llms` to print this reference.

IMPORTANT: You operate INSIDE a session created by the user. Never create
sessions yourself — that is the user's job. Use `tv tag` to identify yourself,
`tv ls` to discover sessions, and exec/peek/history to interact with them.

## Core Workflow: Peek → Act → Verify

Every interaction follows this cycle:

  tv peek @s -j                              1. check current state
  tv exec @s "command" {cr} {wait:5s}        2. act
  tv peek @s -j                              3. verify the result

Sessions are shared — the user or other agents may be actively using them.
Always peek before sending keystrokes to avoid interfering with work in
progress. Never assume success without checking.

## Session Identification

- **Hex ID**: 4-char hex (e.g. `a1b2`). Prefix matching works.
- **Tag**: `@name` (e.g. `@work`). Set with `tv tag @name`.
- **Omitted**: targets your own session (resolved via process ancestry).

## Commands

### tv ls
List active sessions, sorted by most recent activity.
  tv ls [-a] [-j] [selector]
  -a    include hidden sessions
  -j    JSON (extra: pid, visible, in_alt_screen, created_at,
        last_activity as epoch, full cwd)

### tv peek / tv p
Read current visible screen content.
  tv peek -j [selector]          JSON with metadata (use this)
  tv peek [selector]             raw text (for piping to grep/tail)
  JSON fields: content, rows, cols, cursor_row, cursor_col, in_alt_screen

### tv history / tv h
Read scrollback history (lines that scrolled off screen + current screen).
  tv history -j [-n count] [-o offset] [selector]
  tv history [-n count] [-o offset] [selector]
  -n count    number of lines from end
  -o offset   skip N lines from end
  JSON fields: content

History captures output that has scrolled off the visible screen. For what's
currently visible, use tv peek.

Note: alt-screen programs (vim, less, htop) do NOT write to history.

### tv exec / tv x
Send keystrokes to a session. Blocks until all keys are delivered.
  tv exec <selector> <keys>...

Always include {cr} to press Enter — without it, text is typed but never runs:
  tv exec @s "ls -la" {cr}              correct
  tv exec @s "ls -la"                   just types, never submits

Braces in typed text must be escaped:
  tv exec @s "echo {{hello}}" {cr}      sends: echo {hello}

### tv tag / tv t
Get, set, or delete session tags.
  tv tag                    show own tag
  tv tag @name              set own tag
  tv tag <selector> @name   set another session's tag
  tv tag -d                 delete own tag

### tv show / tv hide
Control session visibility in tv ls.
  tv show [selector]
  tv hide [selector]

### tv clear
Clear scrollback and history buffer.
  tv clear [selector]

## Key Syntax

Each argument to `tv exec` can mix literal text and {expressions}.
A configurable delay (default 50ms) is inserted between separate arguments.

### Key Names
  {cr} {enter}      Enter/Return
  {esc}             Escape
  {tab} {s-tab}     Tab / Shift+Tab
  {bs} {backspace}  Backspace
  {space}           Space
  {del} {delete}    Delete
  {up} {down} {left} {right}   Arrow keys
  {home} {end} {insert}        Navigation
  {pageup} {pagedown}          Page Up/Down
  {f1}..{f12}                  Function keys
  {nul}                        NUL byte (0x00)
  {stdin}                      piped input (see "Writing Files" below)

### Modifiers
  {c-KEY}     Ctrl       e.g. {c-c} {c-d} {c-z} {c-a}
  {a-KEY}     Alt/Meta   e.g. {a-x} {a-f}
  {s-KEY}     Shift      e.g. {s-tab} {s-up}
  Combinable: {c-s-up} for Ctrl+Shift+Up

### Escaped Braces
  {{ → literal {      }} → literal }

## Timing

### {wait:DURATION} — Smart Wait (use by default)

Watches terminal output and returns early once it settles. Falls back to
DURATION as a hard maximum.

  {wait:10s}       wait up to 10s, idle threshold 1s (default)
  {wait:30s:2s}    wait up to 30s, idle threshold 2s
  {wait:2m:5s}     wait up to 2 minutes, idle threshold 5s

Always use {wait} after commands that produce output:
  tv exec @s "make build" {cr} {wait:60s}
  tv exec @s "npm install" {cr} {wait:2m}
  tv exec @s "cargo test" {cr} {wait:2m:3s}

For bursty output (builds, installs), increase the idle threshold to avoid
returning during a gap between output chunks.

### {sleep:DURATION} — Fixed Delay

Always waits exactly the specified time. No output monitoring.
Use only when there is no output to detect:
  tv exec @s "ssh prod" {cr} {sleep:3s}        connection setup
  tv exec @s "vim file" {cr} {sleep:500ms}      app startup

### Duration Syntax
  500ms  2s  1m  1h  1m30s  (bare number = milliseconds)

## Writing Files and Multiline Content

For anything beyond simple one-liners, pipe content via {stdin}. This avoids
brace escaping, manual {cr} insertion, and special character issues.

### How {stdin} Works

{stdin} reads all piped input and inserts it as keystrokes at its position in
the key sequence. Newlines in the piped text become Enter keystrokes. One
trailing newline is automatically stripped (since echo/heredocs always add one).
Can appear once per command. Requires piped input.

### The File Writing Pattern

To write a file in a session, use a shell heredoc in the target session and
pipe the content via {stdin}:

  printf 'hello world' | tv exec @s "cat > file.txt << 'EOF'" {cr} {stdin} {cr} EOF {cr}

Breaking it down:
  "cat > file.txt << 'EOF'" {cr}    types the heredoc command + Enter
  {stdin}                            inserts piped content (newlines → Enter)
  {cr}                               Enter after the last line of content
  EOF {cr}                           closes the heredoc

### Multiline Files

For content with quotes, special characters, or many lines, use a heredoc on
the piping side too:

  cat << 'INPUT' | tv exec @s "cat > deploy.sh << 'EOF'" {cr} {stdin} {cr} EOF {cr}
  #!/bin/bash
  set -e
  echo "deploying ${APP_NAME}"
  docker-compose up -d
  INPUT

No escaping needed at any level — braces, quotes, $variables, and special
characters are all sent verbatim. Use different delimiters for the outer
heredoc (INPUT) and inner heredoc (EOF).

### Simple Cases

For short single-line content, regular exec works fine:
  tv exec @s "echo hello > file.txt" {cr} {wait:2s}

## Output Processing

Default output is clean raw text. Use -j (JSON) when you need structured
data or metadata. Use plain output when piping to grep/tail/head.

  tv peek @s -j | jq -r .content          extract content from JSON
  tv peek @s -j | jq .in_alt_screen       check if in a TUI
  tv peek @s -j | jq .cursor_row          read cursor position
  tv peek @s | grep -i error              search screen for errors
  tv peek @s | tail -5                    last 5 lines on screen
  tv history @s -n 500 | grep FAIL        search scrollback
  tv ls -j | jq '.[].tag'                list all tags

Errors go to stderr — piped stdout is always clean.

## Alt-Screen Programs (vim, less, htop)

Some programs use an alternate screen buffer:
  tv peek    → shows alt-screen content (what's currently visible)
  tv history → returns scrollback from BEFORE alt-screen entry only
  tv peek -j → "in_alt_screen" tells you which mode you're in

## Agent Workflows

### Setup
  tv tag                                    see your own session ID and tag
  tv tag @agent                             tag yourself for identification
  tv ls                                     discover all active sessions

### Run a Command and Read Output
  tv peek @s -j                             check state first
  tv exec @s "ls -la" {cr} {wait:5s}
  tv peek @s -j                             read the result

### Build and Check for Errors
  tv exec @s "cargo build" {cr} {wait:2m:5s}
  tv peek @s | grep -i error                search visible screen
  tv history @s -n 200 | grep -i error      search full output

### Write a File, Run It, Verify
  cat << 'INPUT' | tv exec @s "cat > /tmp/test.py << 'EOF'" {cr} {stdin} {cr} EOF {cr}
  import json
  data = {"status": "ok", "count": 42}
  print(json.dumps(data, indent=2))
  INPUT
  tv exec @s "python3 /tmp/test.py" {cr} {wait:5s}
  tv peek @s -j
  tv exec @s "rm /tmp/test.py" {cr} {wait:2s}

### Chain Multiple Commands
  tv exec @s "cd /app" {cr} {wait:2s} "npm install" {cr} {wait:30s}

### Interrupt a Stuck Process
  tv exec @s {c-c}
  tv peek @s -j                             verify it stopped

### Navigate a TUI
  tv peek @s -j                             check in_alt_screen first
  tv exec @s {esc} ":wq" {cr}              vim: save and quit
  tv exec @s {esc} ":q!" {cr}              vim: quit without saving
  tv exec @s "q"                            less/man: quit
  tv exec @s {c-d}                          exit shell / EOF

### Search Long Output
  tv exec @s "cargo test" {cr} {wait:60s}
  tv history @s -n 500 | grep "FAILED"      find failures
  tv history @s -n 500 | tail -20           see summary

### Monitor a Long-Running Process
  tv exec @s "npm run dev" {cr} {sleep:2s}
  tv peek @s | grep "ready"                 check later

### Work With SSH
  tv exec @s "ssh user@host" {cr} {sleep:3s}
  tv peek @s -j                              verify connected
  tv exec @s "ls" {cr} {wait:5s}
  tv peek @s -j

## Best Practices

1. Peek before you act — check what's on screen before sending keystrokes.
2. Sessions are shared — the user may be typing. Don't blindly send keys.
3. Always add {cr} after commands — they won't run without Enter.
4. Always {wait} after commands that produce output — don't guess timing.
5. Peek after important operations — never assume success.
6. Use -j for structured data, plain output for piping to grep/tail.
7. Use {wait:D:T} with a longer idle threshold for bursty output.
8. Use {sleep} only when there's no terminal output to detect.
9. Use tv history when output scrolled off screen — peek only shows what's visible.
10. Use {stdin} for multiline content — avoids all escaping problems.

## Configuration

File: ~/.config/tv/config.toml

  default_visibility = "visible"   # or "hidden"
  default_permission = "prompt"    # or "allow" / "deny"
  output_format = "pretty"         # or "json"
  history_size = 10000             # scrollback lines retained
  send_delay_ms = 50               # delay between exec arguments (ms)
  idle_threshold_ms = 1000         # default idle threshold for {wait} (ms)
  log_level = "info"               # daemon log level
